summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the overall balanced chemical equation for photosynthesis?

The overall equation for photosynthesis summarizes the conversion of six molecules of carbon dioxide and six molecules of water, using light energy, into one molecule of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) and six molecules of oxygen. This process actually occurs in two stages: the light-dependent reactions (thylakoids) produce ATP and NADPH, and the Calvin cycle (stroma) uses these to fix CO₂ into organic sugar.

What is the source of the oxygen gas (O₂) released as a byproduct during photosynthesis?

The oxygen released during photosynthesis comes from the photolysis (light-splitting) of water at photosystem II. This was definitively proven by Cornelis van Niel's hypothesis and later confirmed by Ruben and Kamen's isotope-tracing experiments using heavy oxygen (¹⁸O). When plants were given H₂¹⁸O, the ¹⁸O appeared in the released O₂ gas, not in the glucose—proving water, not CO₂, is the oxygen source.
